meta.title: A Day in the Life: UK Property Placement
meta.subtitle: Walk through each of the 13 workflow stages from your perspective — from case creation through transition to servicing. See today’s pain, the future experience, what you do, what’s automated, and how data flows across the end-to-end placement lifecycle.
meta.facilitationGuide: For each stage: (1) Start with 🔴 Today’s Reality — validate the pain points. (2) Switch to 🟢 Future Experience — is this the right vision? (3) Review ✋ My Actions — what’s missing? (4) Check ⚡ Workflow Automates — is the automation level right? (5) Review 📊 Data Domains and 🖥️ Systems — are these correct? Use 👥 Other Performers to confirm team interactions.
meta.sourceData: Current-state step counts from Gallagher program context. Future-state from UK Property Placement Future State V2 process maps, E2E Workflow Stage Diagrams, and Workflow Engine Proposed Stages (13-stage model, March 2026). Data domains from Data Flow End to End workbook (Sheet 2: UK Property Placement). Systems from As-Is Redesign and Future State diagrams. Process step counts based on Future State and Redesign process maps (not based on detailed tasks).

broker.name: Broker: Sarah Mitchell (composite persona)
broker.ctx: Sarah manages a portfolio with multiple UK property cedants. Her priority is delivering fast, high‑quality market engagement for her clients while preserving strong relationships and competitive outcomes; her day revolves around Outlook, client calls, yet today she is weighed down by fragmented systems, repeated data entry, and manual tracking. She is frustrated by administrative tasks that dilute her broking time, and welcomes tools that remove friction without adding extra clicks.

support.name: Broker Support: James Chen (composite persona)
support.ctx: James focuses on data accuracy, compliance evidence, and smooth system handoffs, but today spends much of his time performing triple data entry, assembling documents manually, and chasing missing information. He wants structured workflows that allow him to act as a quality‑assurance specialist rather than a data‑entry operator.

broker.story: Sarah opens an email from her cedant with a renewal submission -  a PDF, two spreadsheets, and a loss history screenshot pasted into the email body. She opens WIN to check the opportunity pipeline, then switches to the team's Pipeline List .xls to enter details, then back to email to forward everything to Broker Support. She saves the email in her email filing structure. If modelling was needed, she triggers the request to the modeling team and have to wait for Cat Analytics to return their output, then manually saves the modelling files alongside the submission. Plenty of admin time, zero broking.
pains: Manual data entry into personal Pipeline List .xls tracker — disconnected from WIN
Weekly review of Pipeline List .xls to track submissions — no real-time visibility
Submission pack arrives in varied formats (email body, PDFs, Excel) — no structured ingestion
Forwards email chain to Broker Support informally — no structured handoff
Must manually request UMR creation, sanctions checks, and market security checks
Same data will be re-keyed into multiple systems/xls file

support.story: James learns about Sarah’s new submission when she forwards an email chain and sends a Teams message saying ‘can you set this up?’ He opens the forwarded thread, hunts for attachments, and begins manually keying data into the Pipeline List .xls. He then cross-references with WIN, and starts entering data into OpenTWINS.
pains: Learns about new submissions informally via forwarded email — no structured notification
Must review weekly Pipeline List .xls file to track work across brokers
Enters details into Pipeline List .xls manually — then re-keys same data into WIN and OpenTWINS
Creates UMR in OpenTWINS manually
Updates UMR in Pipeline List .xls after OpenTWINS creation — another manual update
No completeness checklist — relies on experience to know which fields matter
Stores ingestion data manually — documents scattered across email filing and shared drives

broker.story: Sarah receives the renewal submission via Outlook. The Opportunity Pipeline in WIN triggers the renewal workflow automatically. She uploads the submission pack to the Workflow Engine, which intelligently ingests the data — extracting structured fields from PDFs and spreadsheets with rules-based validation. The system creates the placement case, populates WIN, flags any missing mandatory fields, and triggers CAT modelling if required. In few minutes the case is live, validated, and ready for compliance screening.
actions: Initiate renewal discussions with Cedant
Receive submission pack via Outlook
Upload submission pack to Workflow Engine
Review and confirm intelligently ingested data
Request additional details from cedant (if required)
automated: Opportunity Pipeline in WIN triggers renewal workflow automatically — no more manual Pipeline List .xls
Auto-create placement case; assign case owner via routing rules
Intelligent Ingestion extracts structured data with rules-based validation, ensures completeness
Intelligent Ingestion stores data within Data Lake and DMS simultaneously
Validate completeness — flag missing mandatory fields
Workflow routes placement to Modelling/Analytics if required
Auto-generate email with information request for Broker if data gaps found
Stage gate blocks progression until required data complete
All emails associated with a Submission/Placement (e.g., tagged with UMR) will be auto-stored within appropriate folder within DMS

support.story: James receives a Workflow Engine notification that a new submission case has been auto-created. The intelligent ingestion has already extracted and validated the data. He opens the pre-populated validation checklist, confirms two low-confidence fields against the source PDF, and verifies the WIN link. No more Pipeline List .xls. No more re-keying into OT. Total time: 8 minutes.
actions: Validate ingestion output against source submission documents
Update data fields where ingestion confidence is low
automated: Notification of new case assignment in Workflow Engine — replaces informal email/Teams
Pre-populated validation checklist based on product type — replaces tribal knowledge
Intelligent Ingestion stores data within Data Lake and DMS — replaces manual document filing
Pipeline List .xls eliminated — Opportunity Pipeline lives in WIN with workflow triggers
Data sync between Workflow Engine, WIN, and OpenTWINS — no re-keying
All emails associated with a Submission/Placement (e.g., tagged with UMR) will be auto-stored within appropriate folder within DMS

steps: Prepare and send submission pack to Broker via Outlook

broker.story: Sarah needs sanctions clearance before she can market the risk. She sends an email to Broker Support asking them to ‘run the checks.’ Then she waits. If it goes AMBER, the email chain with the Sanctions team stretches over days. There’s no status.
pains: Requests sanctions checks via email to Broker Support
No visibility into check status — relies on inbox monitoring and chasers
Sanctions clearance might delay market engagement
Must manually upload evidence and artefacts to e-filing and storage repository (GEM)
AMBER/RED outcomes trigger unstructured email chains with Sanctions team

support.story: James receives Sarah’s email requesting checks. He manually conducts the sanctions check in SIT and downloads the report. If it comes back GREEN — but he still needs to upload evidence to e-filing, update the artefacts in the storage repository for GEM. If it's RED/AMBER — he has to complete an SRT record and chase the Sanctions Team. Similarly he has to complete Market Security screening in Guard and follow up with the Market Security Team for not approved Markets. It’s a very manual process with multiple GEM touchpoints and no structured workflow.
pains: Manually conducts sanctions check in SIT and downloads report
If AMBER/RED: completes SRT record, forwards to Sanctions Team
Updates Sanctions compliance status in Pipeline List .xls — yet another manual update
Perform manually Market Security checks in Guard and engage with Market Security Team for not approved Markets.
Email exchanges/follow up with Market Security Team
Uploads evidence to e-filing and artefacts into storage repository — manual GEM touchpoints

broker.story: Sarah’s Workflow Engine dashboard shows a green tick — sanctions screening passed automatically. The evidence has been auto-documented for GEM. She didn’t send a single email or open a single additional system. If the case was AMBER/RED, the referral was auto-created in SRT and routed to the Sanctions Team with all context attached.
actions: Review sanctions outcome (Human in the Loop) — if AMBER/RED
automated: Initiate sanctions screen against checklist via SIT automatically
Tri-state routing: GREEN → auto-pass; AMBER/RED → SRT referral
Auto-create referral in SRT for AMBER/RED outcomes
Document evidence and artefacts in accordance with GEM
Update Sanctions check status in the Opportunity Pipeline in WIN
All emails associated with a Submission/Placement (e.g., tagged with UMR) will be auto-stored within appropriate folder within DMS

support.story: James sees the sanctions result auto-populated on his Workflow dashboard — GREEN for Sarah’s case. The evidence is already filed in DMS for GEM. For the AMBER case earlier, the workflow auto-created the SRT record and routed it to the Sanctions team with all context attached.
actions: Review sanctions and request details from Broker if Sanctions review fails (if AMBER/RED)
automated: Sanctions check automated in SIT — no manual login, search, or download
SRT record auto-created for AMBER/RED with context attached
Workflow routes to Sanctions Team automatically with SLA timer
Evidence documented in GEM automatically — no manual upload
All emails associated with a Submission/Placement (e.g., tagged with UMR) will be auto-stored within appropriate folder within DMS
Market Security screening automatically triggered/completed at Draft Slip Creation stage

otherPerformers.name: Sanctions Team
steps: Sanctions Review Process (structured workflow with SLA)
Receive auto-created SRT referral with full context (AMBER/RED only)

dataDomains.note: Screening results, SRT records, evidence for GEM
Cedant screened against sanctions lists
Carriers sanctions compliance status
UMR assigned, compliance status linked

broker.story: Sanctions are clear. Sarah now needs to prepare marketing materials, engage markets, and evaluate responses — all manually. She opens a previous marketing email as a template, manually updates the risk details, and sends the submission to 6 target reinsurers via individual Outlook emails. Over the next hours, responses trickle in — some via email, some via phone. She tracks who has responded in a personal spreadsheet, compares quotes across emails and phone notes, and prepares a quote letter for the Cedant. If modelling was needed, she waits for Cat Analytics to return their output before sending the submsision pack to Market.
pains: Manually prepares marketing email using previous emails as informal templates — no standardised communication pack
Manual email distribution to each market — no structured routing or Quote ID system
Tracks responses in personal spreadsheet — no unified view of market engagement
No automated reminders or deadline tracking for market responses
Negotiations tracked informally — in head and in inbox
Manual quote comparison across emails, spreadsheets, and phone notes
Waits for modelling output with no tracking of when it will arrive

support.story: James doesn’t have a structured role in marketing or market engagement today — but he often gets pulled in ad-hoc to help format documents, chase modelling outputs, and check that the submission pack is complete. He has no visibility into which markets have responded, what terms are discussed, or whether cases are on track.
pains: No structured task in marketing prep — pulled in ad-hoc by brokers
Chases Cat Analytics informally for modelling outputs
No visibility into market engagement status — learns about outcomes informally
Cannot prepare for next workflow stage proactively
No standardised ‘market-ready’ checklist

broker.story: With sanctions compliance clear, the system has already determined whether modelling is needed and triggered the Cat Analytics workflow in parallel at case creation. The auto-populated communication pack is ready — pre-filled with risk details, terms, and the standard email template. Sarah reviews, adjusts, and sends. All emails include a workflow-generated Quote ID in the subject. Responses are tracked by the workflow: she can see at a glance who’s quoting, who’s declined. She focuses on negotiations — the one thing the workflow explicitly does not automate. She evaluates quotes, prepares a quote letter, and sends terms to the cedant.
actions: Review and adjust auto-populated communication pack
Send marketing email and submission pack to Markets via Outlook
Conduct negotiations with Markets and Cedant (human-driven — not automated)
Evaluate quotes and terms across markets
Prepare quote letter and terms details; send to Cedant
automated: Route to Modelling/Analytics if required (parallel workflow triggered at case creation); store analytics outputs in DMS; update Pipeline in WIN
Auto-populate communication pack and standard email
Emails include workflow-generated Quote ID in subject
Store emails with Quote ID in DMS (Harmon.ie pop-up permission)
Track inbound market responses; classify (Accept / Decline)
Auto-send decline notifications to rejected markets
Maintain response timestamp and audit trail
All emails associated with a Submission/Placement (e.g., tagged with UMR) will be auto-stored within appropriate folder within DMS

support.story: For the first time, James has real-time visibility into marketing and market engagement. He can see on his Workflow dashboard which cases are in active negotiation, which have received quotes, and which are approaching firm order. He initiates Placement Structure preparation and confirms submission data in the Placement Tool.
actions: Initiate Placement Structure preparation (Workbench)
Confirm submission data, populate market info (Placement Tool)
Prepare marketing email
automated: Communication pack auto-populated from submission data and templates
Modelling outputs auto-stored and associated with placement in DMS
Real-time visibility into market engagement status (new — previously invisible)
Quote ID tracking and email filing via DMS
Auto-notification when case transitions to next workflow stage
All emails associated with a Submission/Placement (e.g., tagged with UMR) will be auto-stored within appropriate folder within DMS

broker.story: Quotes are in and Sarah has presented her recommendation to the cedant. Now she waits for confirmation. She tracks this informally — checking her inbox, making follow-up calls. Once the cedant confirms, she sends a ‘proceed’ email to Broker Support. If declined, she notifies markets manually. The handoff to Draft Slip is a forwarded email.
pains: No structured tracking of cedant decision status — relies on inbox and memory
Must manually follow up with cedant for confirmation
Transition to Draft Slip is an email forward — no structured trigger
Pipeline .xls must be manually updated with outcome
No automated decline notifications to rejected markets if cedant declines

support.story: James updates the Pipeline List .xls with the outcome, uploads evidence to the storage repository, and waits for the broker’s forwarded email telling him to initiate slip preparation. He has no visibility into when the cedant decision is expected.
pains: Updates Pipeline .xls with outcome manually
Uploads evidence and artefacts to storage repository for GEM manually
Waits for broker email to initiate next stage — no structured trigger
No visibility into cedant decision timeline

broker.story: Sarah receives the cedant’s confirmation via the workflow — or handles a decline. The system auto-generates structured communication templates. On confirmation, the workflow triggers transition to Draft Slip Creation automatically. All documentation is updated in WIN and DMS. No more manual Pipeline .xls updates.

Notify Cedant — Term Agreed
Update Opportunity Pipeline in WIN and documentation in DMS

Attach quote letter / terms; log delivery + read confirmation

support.story: The moment Sarah’s cedant confirms, James receives an automatic notification with all case data pre-loaded. The workflow has already classified the case and all data flows into the next stage — no re-keying required.

broker.story: Sarah sends a ‘proceed’ email to Broker Support with instructions to start the draft slip. She has to specify whether it’s new business, renewal, or endorsement. When the draft arrives, she reviews it in Word — marking up changes, checking terms against her negotiation notes, and emailing feedback. If terms need amendment, the loop repeats. Version control relies on filenames and email dates.
pains: Initiates draft slip via email forward — no structured trigger
Must manually specify new business/renewal/endorsement classification
References previous placements informally — no systematic template selection
Reviews draft slip in Word via email — no structured review workflow
Version control relies on filenames and email dates
Feedback loop between Broker and Broker Support is manual and slow
No digital signatures or approval capture

support.story: James receives the email from Sarah and determines the classification. For renewal cases, he searches shared drives for last year’s slip, manually populates it with data from the submission. For new business cases, he checks pipeline list .xls for similar cases to create a first draft. In both cases, he sends the draft when ready by email to Sarah for review. When feedback comes back, he makes amendments and re-routes. Each iteration requires manual document management.
pains: Receives initiation informally — must interpret broker’s email
Manually determines classification (new/renewal/endorsement)
Creates draft slip manually — pulling data from multiple sources
Uses previous slip as informal template — inconsistent quality
Document management is manual — filenames, storing, and email tracking
No audit trail of changes and approvals

broker.story: The workflow has automatically classified Sarah’s case as a renewal and triggered the Draft Slip Creation workflow. The Contract Builder auto-generates a draft slip using the appropriate template with all submission data pre-populated. Sarah reviews it within the workflow — she can see tracked changes, comment inline, and approve directly. The system maintains version control and a full audit trail with GEM compliance auto-documented.
actions: Initiate placement Structure preparation (Workbench)
Review and complete draft slip
Review Pass? — approve or request amendments
automated: Classify case: New Business / Renewal / Endorsement
Retrieve historical placement data (automated historical check)
Initiate Slip production: Contract Builder + Data Lake
Apply template logic based on classification
Cross-reference markets with approved list in Guard; document evidence
Route draft slip through review process; document records (GEM)
Capture digital approval; log audit trail
All emails associated with a Submission/Placement (e.g., tagged with UMR) will be auto-stored within appropriate folder within DMS

support.story: James receives a structured workflow notification. The case is pre-classified as a renewal. He initiates the draft slip using Contract Builder and standard templates, with submission data pre-populated. Amendments are version-controlled with delta tracking. James also initiates/triggers Market Security screening via Guard. The workflow captures all outcomes and evidence automatically.

broker.story: Once the draft is internally approved, Sarah manually assembles the package/email to send to markets. She emails the draft slip to each market individually, then tracks their responses informally. If a market rejects the terms, she handles the declinature review manually and may need to loop back to the Cedant for material changes.If changes are required, Sarah emails Broker Support with the required updates. Further revisions handled via email exchanges.
pains: Manually assembles market package from multiple document sources
Manual Contra-Sign of the draft-slip documentation
Tracks market feedback on draft terms informally
No structured dispatch process — emails sent individually
Declinature handling is unstructured
Material changes require manual Cedant communication
Changes/updates handled via email exchanges between Broker and Broker Support — no structured workflow

support.story: James has limited visibility into the market dispatch process. He learns about market responses informally from the Broker and has no structured way to track whether markets have responded. If any changes are needed after market feedback, he waits for the broker to email him with instructions. He cannot prepare proactively for the next stage because he has no visibility into when market feedback will come or what it will be.
pains: No visibility into market dispatch or response status
Learns about market feedback informally from Broker via email
No structured process for tracking draft slip responses
Cannot prepare proactively for next stage

broker.story: The workflow automatically generates an outbound communication with the approved draft slip attached. Sarah triggers the dispatch via via PPL or email with tracked timestamps. Market responses and feedback are tracked centrally. If a market rejects, the declinature review process is structured. If material changes are requested by Markets, Sarah notifies Cedant and completes the necessary negotiations. Sarah instructs any required changes (material or not) to Broker Support.

Send prepared communication to Markets - trigger dispatch via PPL or email
Handle Declinature Review (if market rejects)
Notify/negotiate with Cedant if material changes requested by Markets
Instruct Broker Support of any required changes to slip (material or not)
automated: Generate outbound communication; attach approved draft
Dispatch via PPL or email; track timestamp

Amend draft slip using Contract Builder based on broker instructions and market feedback
automated: Market dispatch tracked with timestamps

broker.story: When the Cedant confirms the firm order, Sarah manually notifies everyone and coordinates the transition to final slip production. She sends acknowledgement email to Cedant and updates her Pipeline .xls. The transition to Final Slip is another informal email handoff.

broker.story: Sarah initiates final slip production by emailing Broker Support with the finalised terms. The Wording Team creates the final slip in Word. She then receives it for peer review — checking it against the draft, her negotiation notes, and the Cedant’s requirements. If changes are needed, she emails the Wording Team with markup. Amendment loops are slow and error-prone.
pains: Initiates final slip production via email — no structured workflow
Must confirm all draft amendments are captured in final version
Final slip created in Word — disconnected from source data
Peer review conducted via email and Word markup — no structured approval chain
No digital signatures or approval capture
Amendment loops slow and error-prone

support.story: James/Wording Team creates the final slip manually from the draft, incorporating final terms. He then coordinates the peer review, manages amendment requests, and tracks who has reviewed and approved. All done via email with no audit trail.

Coordinates review via email — no structured routing

broker.story: Once Sarah confirm that firm order has been received, the workflow auto-initiates final slip production. The Digital Contract Builder generates the final slip from all agreed terms — pre-populated with the exact data from the negotiation. Sarah reviews it through a structured peer review process within Contract Builder, captures her approval digitally. If amendments are needed, the workflow routes it to the Wording Team for redrafting iterations with full audit trail.

If a market rejects at this stage, she handles the declinature review informally (emails, her personal notes). Signatures are tracked in her inbox.

Must manually trigger Post Bind stage
No automated document archival

broker.story: Once all signatures are in, the workflow auto-saves all documents, generates the Cedant version, and prepares a draft email for Sarah to send to the Cedant and confirms binding. The Post Bind stage is triggered seamlessly.
actions: Validate that all signatures have been received
Send email to Cedant (drafted by system) with final slip attached and binding confirmation
automated: Store final artefacts; sync DMS and Data Lake

broker.story: After binding, Sarah has minimal direct involvement in post-bind validation — but she needs to ensure the Post Bind checklist is covered. She relies on Broker Support to verify that all evidence is filed and GEM compliance touchpoints are complete.

No automated check against documentation generated throughout the process
Missing documents trigger manual chasers

support.story: James manually cross-references the checklist against saved documentation, updates evidence to e-filing, and completes the Post Bind checklist for GEM. If documents are missing, he chases the broker. He emails Global FAC Processing with final slip and all necessary documents.

Completes Post Bind checklist manually (GEM)

Manual notification (email) to Global FAC Processing, no automatic routing

broker.story: Sarah receives final Post Bind artefacts (Final slip, evidence of cover and debit notes) from Broker Support for her final review. If the review is successful, she sends the final Post Bind artefacts to Cedant via email. The review process is informal — approvals tracked via email, no structured sign-off.
pains: Review and sign-off is informal
Sends Post Bind artefacts to Cedant manually via email
No structured approval chain

support.story: James coordinates the Second Pair Of Eyes (SPOE) review, manages amendments if the review fails, and sends artefacts to the Broker for final review and dispatch to Cedant. He also coordinates the GEM review and ensures all sign-offs are captured — all via email.
pains: Coordinates SPOE review informally via email
Routes amendments manually if review fails
Dispatches artefacts to cedant manually
Captures sign-offs manually — no digital audit trail

broker.story: The workflow routes Post Bind artefacts for SPOE Review with structured sign-off. Sarah reviews and approves on her dashboard. On approval, the workflow prepares draft email with the artefacts for Sarah to send to the Cedant.

Broker Review Pass? — approve or return
Send Post Bind Artefacts to Cedant

support.story: James sends artefacts for SPOE review through the workflow. Reviews are routed automatically with digital sign-off. If the review fails, amendments are routed back through the structured workflow. On approval, artefacts are dispatched to the cedant.
